questionText: Find a quadratic equation, with integer coefficients, whose roots are $3$ and $-2$.

steps.description: Write each root as a factor.
steps.explanation: If $x = r$ is a root, then $(x - r)$ is a factor; so the roots give the factors $(x-3)$ and $(x+2)$.

steps.description: Form the product of the factors.
steps.explanation: A quadratic with these roots is the product of the two factors set equal to zero.
Multiplying out all four products gives these terms.
Setting the expression to zero gives a quadratic equation with integer coefficients.
The sum of the roots equals $-b/a$ and the product equals $c/a$, both of which match, confirming the equation.
Substituting the root $x = 3$ into the quadratic gives $0$, confirming it is satisfied (and $x = -2$ works too).

steps.description: Note that other quadratics also work.
steps.explanation: Multiplying the whole equation by any nonzero constant keeps the same roots, so this is one of infinitely many answers.
A suitable quadratic equation is $x^2 - x - 6 = 0$.
